ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general green tea beverage class (FOODON_03301844); no specific bayberry-flavoured green tea entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 AN000AJ covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific bayberry green tea sub-code exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code for bayberry green tea beverage; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One large cup (700 g) of bayberry green tea, as typically served by a bubble tea or beverage stall in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (HPB Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for bayberry green tea exists in the HPB database. Values are adapted from HPB data for sweetened green tea beverages (approximately 40 kcal/100 g, ~10 g carbohydrate/100 g) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for sweetened green tea (FDC ID 1104262). The bayberry flavouring contributes negligible additional macronutrients beyond the sugar syrup already accounted for. Nutrient values are consistent with a lightly sweetened tea at approximately 9 g sugar/100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — sweetened tea / sugar-sweetened beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bayberry green tea. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweetened tea and sugar-sweetened beverages with sucrose as the primary sweetener (GI ~55–65 range for sucrose-sweetened drinks). The dominant glycaemic driver is the added sugar syrup (~9 g sugar/100 g). Glycemic load per large serving (700 g): GL = (55 × 70 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 38.5. This is a high glycemic load per serving despite a low GI, due to the large serving volume. GI of 55 is considered low according to the University of Sydney GI Database classification (GI < 55 = low, 56-69 = medium, >70 = high).

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage — 9 g sugar/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g beverage threshold; added sugar drives insulin-mediated sodium retention independently of dietary sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage in its standard sweetened form; request an unsweetened or zero-sugar version, as the added sugar (9 g/100 g) independently elevates blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention per AHA 2018 guidelines, despite the very low sodium content of 2 mg/100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage — 9 g sugar/100 g with negligible fibre (0.1 g/100 g); liquid sugar causes rapid postprandial glucose excursion; glycemic load of 38.5 per large serving is clinically significant
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage; the 9 g sugar/100 g with negligible fibre and a glycemic load of 38.5 per large cup (700 g) will cause acute postprandial blood glucose spikes — request a plain unsweetened green tea with fresh bayberry and no added syrup as a safe alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar intake from regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and increased cardiovascular risk; green tea polyphenols (EGCG) provide a modest counterbalancing benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this sweetened beverage; regular consumption of sugar-sweetened drinks raises triglycerides and lowers HDL cholesterol — switching to unsweetened green tea would preserve the cardioprotective polyphenol (EGCG) benefit without the added sugar risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (9 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; green tea EGCG is anti-inflammatory but benefit is negated by the sugar load in this sweetened form
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should switch to plain unsweetened green tea to obtain the anti-inflammatory EGCG polyphenol benefit without the pro-inflammatory added sugar load present in this sweetened bayberry version.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bayberry Green Tea (Large) is a popular cold sweetened tea beverage sold at Singaporean bubble tea and beverage stalls, typically served in a 700 ml large cup over ice. It is made by brewing green tea, then blending it with bayberry (yangmei, 杨梅) flavour — usually in the form of bayberry syrup or concentrate — and sweetening the mixture with added sugar syrup before chilling. The resulting drink is light reddish-green in colour with a refreshing sweet-tart flavour profile: the grassy, slightly astringent character of green tea is balanced by the fruity, jammy tartness of bayberry. Bayberry (Myrica rubra) is a small red stone fruit native to China, prized for its sweet-sour taste and traditional use in Chinese herbal beverages. Nutritionally, this beverage provides approximately 40 kcal per 100 g, with carbohydrates as the dominant macronutrient (10 g/100 g), of which 9 g/100 g is sugar — almost entirely from the added syrup. Protein (0.2 g/100 g) and fat (0.1 g/100 g) are negligible. Sodium is very low at 2 mg/100 g. A full large serving (700 g) delivers approximately 280 kcal and 70 g of carbohydrates, with a glycemic load of approximately 38.5 — clinically significant for blood glucose management. Green tea contributes trace amounts of catechins (EGCG) with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, but these benefits are substantially offset by the high added sugar content. This beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is not recommended for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease in its standard sweetened form. An unsweetened version with fresh bayberry and no added syrup would be a healthier alternative. The drink is vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a large bayberry green tea?
Can diabetics drink bayberry green tea?
Is bayberry green tea high in sugar?
Is bayberry green tea suitable for hypertension?
What is the glycemic index of sweetened green tea?
How much sugar is in a large cup of bayberry green tea?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for bayberry green tea exists in the HPB Singapore or USDA FoodData Central databases. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB sweetened green tea data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central sweetened tea entries (FDC ID 1104262). The macronutrient profile is consistent and plausible for a lightly sweetened tea at 9 g sugar/100 g. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sucrose-sweetened beverages. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile data for this sweetened tea beverage is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific product category. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific sweetened bayberry green tea beverage are not reported in the HPB Singapore database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no trans fats expected in a tea-based beverage with no dairy or hydrogenated fat). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-based beverage with no animal-derived ingredients). Protein (0.2 g/100 g) and fat (0.1 g/100 g) are non-zero trace values consistent with green tea leaf extraction and are not zero. Two portions are recorded in the input: 1 large cup (700 g, default) and 1 medium-sized beverage cup (500 g); the large cup (700 g) is used as the serving basis for glycemic load calculation. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for sucrose-sweetened beverages; no direct GI measurement for bayberry green tea was found in approved sources. The input listed 'is_default: true' for both portions, which is inconsistent; the large cup (700 g) is treated as the primary default for this large-size record.
